title: NIS2 Art.23 — Significant Incident Reporting Package Completeness and Timeline Control
industry: All sectors in NIS2 Annex I and II
exclusions: Does not perform legal classification of whether an incident meets the Art.23(1) significance threshold (the lookup encodes the operator's pre-classification). Does not draft report content, does not submit reports to the CSIRT or competent authority, does not determine the report format (encoded in the Implementing Regulation template), and does not replace counsel review of cross-border or sector-specific notification requirements.
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.2.49` row-per-missing-stage output, `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:saved_search` archive (7-year retention, RFC 3161 TSA-signed). Dashboard `compliance/nis2-art23-package.xml` — per-incident heatmap and sortable table. Weekly PDF digest to Head of IR.

compliance.assurance_rationale: The UC aggregates evidence from all Art.23 sub-UCs (24h, 72h, intermediate, final, cross-border, public disclosure) into a single per-incident reporting package, proving that each mandatory report stage was produced or tracked. Actual filings and legal classification remain outside Splunk.
compliance.controlObjective: Per significant incident, proves that every Art.23 mandatory report stage (early-warning, notification, intermediate updates, final report, cross-border notices, public disclosure assessment) has a tracked artefact, an assigned owner, and a deadline status.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search `UC-22.2.49` writing one row per (incident, report_stage) gap to `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:saved_search`. Dashboard `NIS2 Art.23 — Package completeness` showing per-incident heatmap.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Tracks whether the initial significant-incident determination was documented and the notification obligation was triggered.
compliance.controlObjective: Confirms that each high/critical ES notable classified as NIS2-significant has a documented significance determination artefact.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Row in the package-completeness search output for stage `significance_determination`.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Tracks that users/recipients were informed when the incident could affect them (Art.23(2) information-to-recipients obligation). Package stage `recipient_notice`.
compliance.controlObjective: Confirms that a recipient-notice artefact was created for incidents meeting the recipient-notification threshold.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Row in the package-completeness search output for stage `recipient_notice`.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Cross-border notification tracked via the `crossborder_assessment` stage.
compliance.controlObjective: Confirms the cross-border impact assessment artefact exists when member_states_affected > 0.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Row in the package-completeness search output for stage `crossborder_assessment`.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Information-to-user obligation tracked via the `recipient_notice` stage.
compliance.controlObjective: Confirms a user-facing notice was drafted when the incident may affect their service.

compliance.assurance_rationale: 24-hour early-warning stage completeness tracked; delegates detection to UC-22.2.1.
compliance.controlObjective: Confirms the 24h early-warning artefact exists for each significant incident.

compliance.assurance_rationale: 72-hour notification stage completeness tracked; delegates detection to UC-22.2.7.
Intermediate update(s) tracked via the `intermediate_update` stage. At least one intermediate update must exist if the incident spans >7 days.
compliance.controlObjective: Confirms that at least one intermediate update artefact exists for long-running incidents.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Final report stage tracked; delegates detection to UC-22.2.8.
compliance.controlObjective: Confirms the final report artefact exists within one month.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Ongoing-incident final-report extension tracked via `final_report_extension` stage.
compliance.controlObjective: Confirms that if the incident is still ongoing at one month, a progress report was filed and the final report deadline was reset.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.23(5) requires the CSIRT to provide guidance and technical advice. The UC tracks whether the operator received and acknowledged guidance, but cannot enforce the CSIRT's action.
compliance.controlObjective: Confirms a `csirt_guidance_ack` artefact was created if the CSIRT issued guidance.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.23(6) authorises the competent authority to order public disclosure. The UC tracks whether a `public_disclosure_assessment` artefact exists.
compliance.controlObjective: Confirms a public-disclosure assessment was documented if required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.23(7) requires the CSIRT or authority to inform ENISA and EU-CyCLONe. The UC tracks the operator's handoff to the CSIRT; ENISA notification is the CSIRT's responsibility.
compliance.controlObjective: Confirms a `csirt_enisa_handoff` artefact documents the handoff.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.23(10) addresses the compilation and anonymisation of aggregate reports by the CSIRT. The UC tracks whether the operator contributed its anonymised incident data.
compliance.controlObjective: Confirms a `anonymised_data_contribution` artefact was generated for CSIRT aggregate reporting.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.23(11) authorises implementing acts on report format. The UC validates that all required fields from the Implementing Regulation template are populated in each artefact.
compliance.controlObjective: Validates report-package artefacts against the Implementing Regulation (EU) 2024/2690 template field list.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Fixture `sample-data/uc-22.2.49-fixture.json` injects: (a) an ES notable with `rule_name=NIS2-Significant-DataBreach`, `urgency=critical`, `nis2_significant=yes`, `_time=now()-80h`, `event_id=SIG-2026-042`; (b) audit_evidence artefacts for `parent_event_id=SIG-2026-042` with stages `early_warning_24h` and `notification_72h` only. The `nis2_reporting_stages.csv` defines 7 required stages for profile=standard. The search returns 5 rows — one per missing stage (intermediate_update, final_report_1m, recipient_notice, public_disclosure_assessment, template_completeness) with `stage_overdue=1` for `intermediate_update` (deadline_hours=168, hours_open=80 < 168 so stage_overdue=0) and `stage_overdue=0` for `final_report_1m` (deadline_hours=720, hours_open=80). Validates that the package correctly identifies gaps.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Same fixture but with all 7 required-stage artefacts present in `index=audit_evidence`. The search returns 0 rows. Validates that a complete package produces no findings.

dataSources: `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:nis2_artifact` for all reporting-stage artefacts written by the upstream UCs (UC-22.2.1, UC-22.2.7, UC-22.2.8, UC-22.2.19, UC-22.2.33) — each artefact has a `stage` field (early_warning_24h, notification_72h, intermediate_update, final_report_1m, crossborder_assessment, recipient_notice, etc.) and a `parent_event_id` linking it to the originating ES notable. `index=notable` via the ES `notable` macro for significant-incident context (rule_name, urgency, status, owner). Lookup `nis2_reporting_stages.csv` defining the required stages per incident profile (columns: `profile`, `stage`, `required`, `deadline_hours`, `description`) — e.g. profile=standard has 7 required stages, profile=crossborder adds 2. Lookup `nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv` from UC-22.2.1 for the significance classification.
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263) ≥7.0; Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621) ≥5.3; Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928) ≥7.7 for ITSM task correlation; Splunk Add-on for Phantom (Splunkbase 3411) ≥3.0 for SOAR artefact correlation.
spl:
`notable` urgency IN ("high","critical") earliest=-90d
| lookup nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv rule_name OUTPUT nis2_tier nis2_significant
| where nis2_significant = "yes"
| eval hours_open = round((now() - _time) / 3600, 1)
| eval incident_profile = if(isnotnull(member_states_affected) AND mvcount(split(member_states_affected, ",")) > 0, "crossborder", "standard")
| rename event_id as parent_event_id
| join type=left parent_event_id [
    search index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:nis2_artifact earliest=-90d
    | stats values(stage) as completed_stages by parent_event_id
  ]
| eval completed_stages = coalesce(completed_stages, "none")
| lookup nis2_reporting_stages.csv profile as incident_profile OUTPUT stage as required_stage, deadline_hours, description as stage_description
| mvexpand required_stage
| eval stage_present = if(mvfind(completed_stages, required_stage) >= 0, 1, 0)
| eval stage_overdue = if(stage_present = 0 AND hours_open > deadline_hours, 1, 0)
| where stage_present = 0
| eval evidence_id = sha256(parent_event_id . required_stage . _time)
| table _time, parent_event_id, rule_name, urgency, nis2_tier, incident_profile, owner, hours_open, required_stage, stage_description, deadline_hours, stage_overdue, evidence_id
| sort - stage_overdue, - hours_open
description: For every ES notable classified as NIS2-significant, expands the required reporting-stage checklist (early-warning, notification, intermediate updates, final report, cross-border assessment, recipient notice, public-disclosure assessment, template validation) and identifies stages where the corresponding evidence artefact in `index=audit_evidence` has not yet been written — surfacing both upcoming stages and overdue ones.
value: Art.23 imposes a multi-stage reporting lifecycle per significant incident. Individual UCs (22.2.1, 22.2.7, 22.2.8) monitor specific clocks, but no single UC confirms the *entire* package is complete. This roll-up UC gives the Head of IR a per-incident checklist view so that a missing stage (e.g. the recipient notice or the anonymised data contribution) is caught before the auditor asks for it.
implementation: (1) Build `nis2_reporting_stages.csv` defining each stage, its deadline, and whether it's required or conditional. (2) Ensure each upstream UC writes an artefact with a `stage` field and `parent_event_id` to `index=audit_evidence`. (3) Schedule every 4 hours over 90 days. (4) Page Head of IR on any `stage_overdue=1`. (5) Email weekly digest of all incomplete packages.
visualization: Heatmap: incidents (rows) × stages (columns), coloured by status (green=present, amber=approaching, red=overdue, grey=not-yet-due). Single-value tiles: incomplete packages count, overdue stages count. Sortable table with drilldown to the individual stage UC.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=true count from datamodel=Alerts where Alerts.severity IN ("high","critical") by Alerts.signature Alerts.severity _time span=1d | rename Alerts.* as * | lookup nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv rule_name AS signature OUTPUT nis2_significant | where nis2_significant="yes"
dataModelAcceleration: Enable `Alerts` DMA as in UC-22.2.1. The CIM variant is not used here because the search is artefact-driven, not event-driven.

references.title: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow on Splunkbase
Splunk Add-on for Phantom on Splunkbase
ENISA NIS2 Technical Implementation Guidance — incident notification lifecycle

knownFalsePositives: 1) **Incident not yet triaged — newly created notable** — all stages show as missing because the IR analyst hasn't started yet. Distinguish: `hours_open < 4` and `status=New` or `status=Unassigned`. Suppress: do NOT suppress — the package starts incomplete by design; only alert on `stage_overdue=1` (which requires the deadline_hours to have passed). 2) **Upstream UC search hasn't fired yet** — the artefact will be written on the next scheduled run of UC-22.2.1/7/8/etc. Distinguish: `stage_overdue=0` (the deadline hasn't passed yet) but the stage is missing. Suppress: the SPL already surfaces only `stage_present=0`; the `stage_overdue` flag differentiates 'not yet due' from 'overdue'. 3) **Planned incident-response exercise (tabletop)** — exercise notables produce a complete set of artefacts with `parent_event_id` prefixed `EXERCISE-`. Distinguish: `parent_event_id` starts with `EXERCISE-` or `TEST-`. Suppress: filter `| where NOT match(parent_event_id, "^(EXERCISE|TEST)-")`. 4) **Standard-profile incident later reclassified as cross-border** — the `incident_profile` was `standard` when the package was first assessed, but cross-border stages were added later. Distinguish: `member_states_affected` populated after initial triage. Suppress: the SPL recalculates `incident_profile` on every run, so the missing cross-border stages appear automatically — no suppression needed, but the Head of IR may see a sudden increase in missing stages. 5) **Duplicate ES notables for the same real-world incident** — two notables with different `event_id` but the same root cause, each generating a partial package. Distinguish: same `risk_object` and `src`/`dest` pair within a 1-hour window. Suppress: consolidate by assigning both notables the same `parent_event_id` in a SOAR playbook; then the artefacts aggregate under one key. 6) **Artefact written to wrong sourcetype or index** — the upstream UC summary-indexed to `index=summary` instead of `index=audit_evidence`. Distinguish: `index=summary sourcetype=evidence:nis2_artifact parent_event_id=<id>` returns results. Suppress: fix the upstream UC's output action to target `index=audit_evidence`; do NOT suppress this finding as it indicates a configuration error.
equipmentModels: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928, incident and task management)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, incident investigation and timeline)
Splunk SOAR 6.x (Splunkbase 3411, reporting package assembly automation)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `audit_evidence` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv`, `nis2_reporting_stages.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv','nis2_reporting_stages.csv')`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.23`. The control objective is: "Per significant incident, proves that every Art.23 mandatory report stage (early-warning, notification, intermediate updates, final report, cross-border notices, public disclosure assessment) has a tracked artefact, an assigned owner, and a deadline status." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `audit_evidence` under `evidence:nis2_artifact`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Build `nis2_reporting_stages.csv` defining each stage, its deadline, and whether it's required or conditional. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

• **Hint 2:** Ensure each upstream UC writes an artefact with a `stage` field and `parent_event_id` to `index=audit_evidence`. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 3:** Schedule every 4 hours over 90 days. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** Page Head of IR on any `stage_overdue=1`. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 5:** Email weekly digest of all incomplete packages. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "NIS2 Art.23 — Significant Incident Reporting Package Completeness and Timeline Control," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself — auditors will read these rows as the official record of NIS2 control activity.

This search correlates the outer dataset (index=`audit_evidence`, sourcetype=`evidence:nis2_artifact`) with a subsearch on `parent_event_id` across the last 90 days. `type=left` preserves all rows from the outer search; nulls in the inner side indicate missing data that may itself constitute a finding.

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Alerts` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=true count from datamodel=Alerts where Alerts.severity IN ("high","critical") by Alerts.signature Alerts.severity _time span=1d | rename Alerts.* as * | lookup nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv rule_name AS signature OUTPUT nis2_significant | where nis2_significant="yes"
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Alerts` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.49: NIS2 Art.23 — Significant Incident Reporting Package Complet]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -90d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.49,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:nis2_artifact earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `_time`, `parent_event_id`, `rule_name`, `urgency`, `nis2_tier`, `incident_profile`, `owner`, `hours_open`, `required_stage`, `stage_description`. Run:

```spl
index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:nis2_artifact earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table _time, parent_event_id, rule_name, urgency, nis2_tier, incident_profile, owner, hours_open
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup nis2_significant_incident_thresholds.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.49: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Heatmap: Incidents (Rows) × Stages (Columns) (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Coloured By Status (`green=present`, `amber=approaching`, `red=overdue`, `grey=not-yet-due`):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Overdue Stages Count. Sortable Table With Drilldown To The Individual Stage Uc (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

• 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

• Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

• Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

• Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: When a serious cyber incident happens, we have to file a whole set of reports to the regulator — an early warning, a full notification, updates, and a final report. This watch checks that we haven't missed any of those reports for any incident, like a teacher checking that every student handed in every homework assignment.
